tracker_summary.inventor: Katalin Karikó and Drew Weissman (key discovery); commercialized via Moderna and BioNTech
tracker_summary.current_use: COVID-19 vaccines; pipeline for influenza, RSV, HIV, cancer immunotherapy, and rare genetic diseases
tracker_summary.initial_funding_source: Hungarian Academy of Sciences (1978–1985); later NIH and American Heart Association grants (repeatedly rejected)
tracker_summary.initial_funding_reason: Basic research into RNA biology and gene therapy; not specifically targeted at vaccines

the_x: Decades of rejected grants, institutional demotion, and basic biochemistry research into how messenger RNA interacts with the innate immune system — work considered a scientific dead end
the_y: Two COVID-19 vaccines authorized in under a year, protecting hundreds of millions of people and demonstrating a universal platform for rapid vaccine development
summary: In 1978, a young Hungarian biochemist named Katalin Karikó became captivated by the idea that synthetic mRNA could instruct human cells to produce their own medicines. For more than two decades, nearly every institution, grant committee, and peer review panel told her she was wrong. She lost her faculty position at UPenn, was demoted, had her salary cut, and watched her NIH grant applications fail year after year. In 2005, collaborating with Drew Weissman, she discovered that substituting pseudouridine for uridine in synthetic mRNA allowed it to slip past the immune system's sentinels. That single insight — published in Immunity and largely ignored — unlocked mRNA as a therapeutic platform. When SARS-CoV-2 emerged in January 2020, both Moderna and BioNTech had optimized mRNA platforms ready. Pfizer-BioNTech received FDA authorization 326 days after the virus's genome was sequenced.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Early RNA-lipid delivery research; state funding cut; smuggled $900 in a teddy bear and emigrated.
chain.the_jump: Without this desperate emigration, the research stops entirely.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Every NIH grant application rejected; UPenn demoted Karikó, cut her salary, told her career was over — same year she was diagnosed with cancer.
chain.the_jump: Most researchers would have left. Karikó stayed, supporting herself on other scientists' projects while continuing mRNA work on the side.
chain.funding: Reduced UPenn salary; no independent grants

chain.what_they_were_doing: Met at a photocopier in the UPenn library and agreed to collaborate with no dedicated funding.
chain.the_jump: A chance encounter at a photocopier initiated the collaboration that would solve the central problem of mRNA therapeutics.

chain.what_they_were_doing: Substituting pseudouridine for uridine prevents immune rejection of synthetic mRNA — published in Immunity, largely ignored.
chain.the_jump: The immune system's rejection was not fundamental but an artifact of unmodified nucleosides. One paper unlocked the entire field.

chain.who: Derrick Rossi; Robert Langer, Noubar Afeyan
chain.what_they_were_doing: Rossi used modified mRNA to reprogram adult cells into stem cells, saw platform potential, co-founded Moderna.
chain.the_jump: A stem cell biologist read an immunology paper ignored by pharma and immediately understood it as a platform technology.

chain.who: Uğur Şahin, Özlem Türeci (BioNTech); Barney Graham, Jason McLellan (NIAID); Moderna
chain.where: Mainz, Germany; NIH Bethesda; Cambridge MA
chain.what_they_were_doing: BioNTech hired Karikó as SVP; NIAID developed prefusion spike stabilization; Moderna ran Phase I trials.
chain.the_jump: Two independent threads — modified mRNA and spike protein stabilization — matured in parallel, ready to be combined the moment a new pathogen appeared.
chain.funding: BioNTech private capital; NIAID intramural; Moderna/BARDA

chain.where: Cambridge MA; Mainz Germany; global trial sites
chain.what_they_were_doing: Within 48 hours of genome release, both companies designed vaccine candidates; Phase III showed 94–95% efficacy; FDA authorized both vaccines by December 18.
chain.the_jump: Forty-two years after Karikó began RNA work in Szeged, the technology she refused to abandon was authorized as the fastest vaccine in human history.
chain.funding: Operation Warp Speed; US government advance purchase agreements

sources: Karikó K, et al. (2005). Suppression of RNA recognition by Toll-like receptors. Immunity, 23(2):165-75. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.immuni.2005.06.008

Warren L, et al. (2010). Reprogramming with synthetic modified mRNA. Cell Stem Cell, 7(5):618-30. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.stem.2010.08.012
